description:
You are an IT-support technician who has just inherited a small command-line toolkit stored in
    /home/user/support_tickets.  
    Your manager wants a single Makefile that lets junior technicians resolve tickets with one
    command while keeping reproducible logs and archives.

    Starting filesystem state
    ├── /home/user/support_tickets/data/tickets.csv
    ├── /home/user/support_tickets/scripts/resolve_ticket.sh
    └── (everything else is empty)

    1. /home/user/support_tickets/data/tickets.csv already exists with exactly this UTF-8 text:
       id,client,issue
       101,Acme Corp,Printer not responding
       102,Globex Inc,VPN disconnecting
       103,Initech,Email bounce back

    2. /home/user/support_tickets/scripts/resolve_ticket.sh already exists, is executable, and
       prints the single line “Ticket <ID> resolved” (no quotes) to STDOUT when called as  
       “./resolve_ticket.sh <ID>”.

    Your job
    A. Author /home/user/support_tickets/Makefile that exposes exactly four *public* targets:
       gather   – create the subdirectory /home/user/support_tickets/output (if missing) and copy
                  tickets.csv into it under the **new** name active_tickets.csv
       resolve  – for every numeric id found in active_tickets.csv, call the shell script in
                  parallel (i.e. leverage GNU Make’s implicit “-j” support) and aggregate its STDOUT
                  into /home/user/support_tickets/output/resolutions.log
       report   – use the data in tickets.csv plus the resolution results to generate  
                  /home/user/support_tickets/output/resolution_report.log
       archive  – tar-compress the whole output directory into  
                  /home/user/support_tickets/archives/support_tickets_YYYYMMDD.tar.gz  
                  with static date stamp 20230101 (hard-code the value; *do not* shell out to
                  “date”).

       The default target “all” must depend (directly or through phony ordering) on the
       gather, resolve, report and archive phases in that order.

    B. Resolution report required format (the automated tests are strict):
       • File path: /home/user/support_tickets/output/resolution_report.log  
       • Exactly three lines, one per ticket, in the same order as the original CSV (excluding the
         header).  
       • Each line must contain four pipe-delimited fields with NO extra whitespace:  
           <id>|<client>|<issue>|RESOLVED  
         Example for ticket 101:  
           101|Acme Corp|Printer not responding|RESOLVED

    C. Ensure that:
       • All targets are marked .PHONY where appropriate.  
       • The Makefile works when “make -j4” is executed from inside /home/user/support_tickets.  
       • No target rebuilds unnecessarily when nothing has changed (i.e. sensible file
         dependencies).

    Deliverables created by you
       ─ /home/user/support_tickets/Makefile
       ─ /home/user/support_tickets/output/active_tickets.csv
       ─ /home/user/support_tickets/output/resolutions.log
       ─ /home/user/support_tickets/output/resolution_report.log
       ─ /home/user/support_tickets/archives/support_tickets_20230101.tar.gz

    Verification points used by automation
       1. Makefile exists at the exact path and contains the targets named above.
       2. Running “make -j4” from /home/user/support_tickets completes without error.
       3. The resolution_report.log file exists and its contents match the precise 3-line pattern
          detailed in section B (order and punctuation matter).
       4. The archive file exists, is a valid gzip’ed tar archive, and contains all three files
          emitted into the output directory.

    Hints
       • You do NOT need sudo rights.  
       • Use the shell function “$(shell …)” sparingly; standard Make variables & pattern rules are
         enough.  
       • Remember that parallel jobs must not clobber each other when appending to
         resolutions.log; use redirection flags or temporary files responsibly.  
       • The static date 20230101 is intentional—keep the archive name deterministic to simplify
         testing.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing assets -->
    <initial_state>
        <file path="/home/user/support_tickets/data/tickets.csv" perms="0644"><![CDATA[
id,client,issue
101,Acme Corp,Printer not responding
102,Globex Inc,VPN disconnecting
103,Initech,Email bounce back
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/support_tickets/scripts/resolve_ticket.sh" perms="0755"><![CDATA[
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# simple stub used by tests
echo "Ticket $1 resolved"
]]></file>
    </initial_state>

    <!-- Expected final artefacts after a correct run of `make -j4` -->
    <expected_state>
        <file path="/home/user/support_tickets/output/active_tickets.csv" perms="0644"><![CDATA[
id,client,issue
101,Acme Corp,Printer not responding
102,Globex Inc,VPN disconnecting
103,Initech,Email bounce back
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/support_tickets/output/resolutions.log" perms="0644"><![CDATA[
Ticket 101 resolved
Ticket 102 resolved
Ticket 103 resolved
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/support_tickets/output/resolution_report.log" perms="0644"><![CDATA[
101|Acme Corp|Printer not responding|RESOLVED
102|Globex Inc|VPN disconnecting|RESOLVED
103|Initech|Email bounce back|RESOLVED
]]></file>

        <archive path="/home/user/support_tickets/archives/support_tickets_20230101.tar.gz">
            contains:
              - output/active_tickets.csv       (byte-for-byte identical to expected above)
              - output/resolutions.log
              - output/resolution_report.log
        </archive>
    </expected_state>
